topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the sickening thud of a dropped book. I stared at the screen, my jaw tightening until my molars ached. 'Regrettably, your application for the Sterling Scholarship has not been selected.' Regrettably. The word tasted like ash in my mouth. I physically leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under the sudden shift of my weight. My hands, which had been hovering over the keyboard with hopeful anticipation just moments before, clenched into tight fists on my thighs. I reread the rejection letter three times, searching for some hidden loophole, some misplaced comma that could somehow justify this utter dismissal. A low, furious hum vibrated in my chest, a pressure building behind my sternum that made my breath catch. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet dorm room. I needed to move. I needed to do something, anything, to shake off this crushing sense of unfairness.

[story 2]

"Denied." The word echoed in the sterile silence of the campus library. I picked up the printed notification, the crisp paper feeling impossibly heavy in my hand. My knuckles were white where I gripped the edges. I could feel the heat rising in my neck, a flush spreading across my cheeks that felt entirely disproportionate to the cold reality of the decision. I stood up abruptly, knocking my chair slightly askew. My voice, when I finally spoke to my roommate, was sharper than intended, laced with a brittle edge. "This is ridiculous. There must have been a mistake." I paced the narrow aisle between the reference section and the periodicals, my footsteps quick and uneven on the polished floor. Every glance I caught from other students felt like an accusation, and I met their eyes with a hard, unyielding stare, refusing to let them see the churning frustration boiling just beneath my calm exterior.

[story 3]

I reread the rejection notice, my eyes scanning the formal language as if it were a coded insult. My lips pressed into a thin, white line. A slow, deliberate exhale escaped me, shaky and ragged. It wasn't just the money; it was the sheer audacity of the rejection. I walked over to the window, pulling the curtain aside just enough to let in a blast of indifferent autumn air. I watched the distant quad, the vibrant green lawns seeming mocking in their perfection. My shoulders tensed, pulling up towards my ears as if bracing for a blow. I turned back, my gaze sweeping over the room—at the untouched textbooks, the neat desk—and a sharp, almost violent impulse took over. I picked up my pen and, with unnecessary force, scribbled a furious, sprawling note across the margin of the rejection letter, a silent testament to the sheer wrongness of the outcome.
